title: John Locke
summary: The English philosopher whose Two Treatises of Government argued that legitimate political authority derives from consent of the governed, that natural rights to life, liberty, and property are inalienable, and that revolution is justified when governments violate those rights — providing the philosophical foundation of liberal democracy and the American Revolution.
hook: When Jefferson wrote 'life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness' he was paraphrasing Locke's 'life, liberty, and estate.' The American founders read Locke as carefully as any text outside the Bible. His argument that government derives its authority from the consent of the governed — and that revolution is justified when government betrays that trust — is the founding document of liberal political philosophy and the most influential political text of the modern world.

beginner.theFigure: John Locke was born in Somerset, England in 1632, during the turbulent decades that would include the English Civil War and the execution of Charles I. He studied at Oxford, became a physician, and attached himself to the household of the Earl of Shaftesbury — one of the most consequential political patrons of the age. When Shaftesbury's political fortunes collapsed, Locke fled to the Netherlands, where he remained until the Glorious Revolution of 1688 brought William of Orange to the English throne. He returned to England in 1689 and published his major works that year: the Essay Concerning Human Understanding, the Two Treatises of Government, and A Letter Concerning Toleration.
beginner.worldOfIdeas:
Locke wrote the Two Treatises to justify the Glorious Revolution — the replacement of the Catholic James II with the Protestant William and Mary. His target was the royalist argument that kings ruled by divine right, as articulated by Robert Filmer's Patriarcha (1680). Filmer argued that political authority derived from God's grant to Adam, transmitted through patriarchal inheritance to kings. Locke demolished this argument in the First Treatise and built an alternative account in the Second.

His starting point was the state of nature — a condition before government in which people are free and equal, governed by natural law derived from reason and God's will. In the state of nature, people have natural rights to life, liberty, and property — derived from the fact that they own themselves and their labor.
beginner.centralIdea:
Locke's argument for political authority runs as follows. In the state of nature, individuals have rights but lack effective enforcement. Property disputes are settled by each party judging their own case — which tends toward conflict. People therefore consent to form a political community, creating government to adjudicate disputes and protect natural rights.

Crucially, this consent is conditional. Government has authority only to protect the rights for which it was created. When government violates those rights — when it becomes tyrannical — the people retain the right to dissolve it and form a new government. This right of revolution is not a license for chaos but a last resort against tyranny. It is the argument that Jefferson adapted in the Declaration of Independence and that the American founders used to justify their revolution.
beginner.howSpread:
Locke's influence on the American founders was direct and documented. Jefferson, Madison, Hamilton, and Adams all read him carefully. The Declaration of Independence's language — 'life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness' — echoes Locke's 'life, liberty, and estate.' The constitutional structure — separation of powers, limited government, the protection of property rights — reflects Lockean principles.

His influence spread through two channels: the explicit use of his arguments by the founders, and the broader shaping of the English liberal tradition through the 18th and 19th centuries. His Essay Concerning Human Understanding argued that the mind at birth is a blank slate shaped by experience. This influenced Enlightenment thinking about education and human improvement.
beginner.contestedInheritance:
C.B. C.B. Macpherson's The Political Theory of Possessive Individualism (1962) mounted the most influential Marxist critique. Locke's property theory was an ideological justification for capitalist property relations, he argued. His 'natural rights' masked class interests. Locke's labor theory of property — which he used to justify original appropriation of land from indigenous peoples — was, Macpherson argued, the philosophical foundation of colonial dispossession.

Locke also held shares in the Royal African Company, which transported enslaved Africans across the Atlantic. His philosophy of natural rights and his direct involvement in the slave trade represent a contradiction that has never been adequately resolved. Whether this makes him a hypocrite or reveals something about liberalism itself is one of the most important debates in the history of political thought.

general.theFigure:
Peter Laslett's critical edition of the Two Treatises of Government (1960) established the modern scholarly understanding of the text — demonstrating through manuscript evidence that the Treatises were written before the Glorious Revolution (probably in 1679–1683, not published until 1689), and that they were connected to Shaftesbury's exclusion crisis rather than simply to justifying the Revolution retrospectively. This discovery substantially changed how the text's political context and purpose are understood.

Jeremy Waldron's The Right to Private Property (1988) and God, Locke, and Equality (2002) provide the most rigorous recent philosophical engagement with Locke's theory of property and rights. James Tully's A Discourse on Property (1980) provided the most important alternative reading — arguing that Locke's property theory is more radically egalitarian than the standard liberal reading suggests.
general.worldOfIdeas:
Locke's epistemology — the Essay Concerning Human Understanding — is foundational for the empiricist tradition that runs through Berkeley and Hume to contemporary analytic philosophy. His argument that all knowledge derives from experience, that the mind at birth is a blank slate, and that complex ideas are built from simple ones derived from sensation and reflection established the framework within which 18th-century debates about knowledge and education were conducted.

His Letter Concerning Toleration (1689) argued for religious toleration on both principled and pragmatic grounds. The principled argument: the state has no authority over conscience, which is a matter between the individual and God. The pragmatic argument: persecution does not produce genuine belief, only external conformity. The toleration Locke argued for was limited — he excluded Catholics (on the grounds of their allegiance to a foreign prince) and atheists (on the grounds that their oaths could not be trusted). But the principle he articulated became the foundation of modern religious liberty.
general.centralIdea:
The labor theory of property is Locke's most influential and most contested contribution. His argument: God gave the world to mankind in common, but each person owns themselves and their labor. When a person mixes their labor with something in the state of nature, they acquire property in it — subject to the provisos that enough remains for others and that nothing is wasted. This argument — which Locke used to justify both individual property rights and colonial appropriation of 'unimproved' land from indigenous peoples — has had enormous consequences.

Locke's argument that indigenous peoples in America had not 'improved' the land through labor, and therefore had no property rights in it, provided the philosophical justification for European colonial land appropriation. This application of his theory — which he made explicitly in the Second Treatise — is one of the most consequential and problematic aspects of his legacy.
general.howSpread:
Locke's influence in France — through Voltaire's enthusiastic promotion of English liberalism, through Montesquieu's The Spirit of the Laws, and through the Encyclopédie — helped shape the Enlightenment's political thought in ways that contributed to the French Revolution. The French Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen (1789) echoes Lockean language about natural rights, though the French tradition interpreted rights differently from the American.

In the 19th century, John Stuart Mill's liberalism developed from a Lockean base while substantially revising it — Mill's harm principle (the only legitimate reason to restrict individual freedom is to prevent harm to others) extends Lockean liberty beyond the property focus. The contemporary liberal tradition — Rawls, Nozick, Dworkin — all engage with Locke as a founding figure, often by arguing against specific Lockean positions.
general.contestedInheritance: The relationship between Locke's philosophy and colonial violence has been one of the most productive debates in contemporary political theory. Barbara Arneil's John Locke and America (1996) showed how extensively Locke's property theory was designed with the American colonial context in mind — that the 'vacant' land argument was central to the project of justifying English colonialism, not an incidental application. This analysis has been extended by postcolonial scholars who argue that liberalism as a tradition is constitutively connected to colonial land appropriation in ways that cannot be separated from the 'universal' principles.

advanced.theFigure: The question of Locke's relationship to the Glorious Revolution and the Whig political tradition has been substantially revised by Laslett's dating of the Two Treatises and by recent work on Shaftesbury's political circle. Mark Goldie's work on Locke's political activities — his involvement in Shaftesbury's plots, his time in exile, his connections to the Whig opposition — provides essential context for the Two Treatises as a political intervention rather than abstract political philosophy.
advanced.worldOfIdeas: The relationship between Locke's empiricism and his political philosophy has been analyzed by scholars who argue they form a coherent system: if all ideas derive from experience, then no one is born with superior insight into natural law or divine will that would justify their authority over others. This democratic epistemology — the radical suggestion that everyone is equally capable of reason and therefore equally capable of self-government — is the deeper connection between the Essay and the Two Treatises.
advanced.centralIdea: Tully's radical reading of Locke's property theory — arguing that the labor theory is designed to show the limits of private property rights rather than to justify capitalist accumulation — represents the most important alternative to the standard liberal reading. On Tully's account, Locke's provisos (enough and as good left for others, no waste) are genuine constraints that would substantially limit capitalist property if applied consistently. Whether Tully's reading is historically accurate or anachronistically applies contemporary concerns to Locke's text is debated.
advanced.howSpread: The global spread of Lockean liberal ideas — through colonialism, through American foreign policy promoting liberal democracy, through international human rights institutions — has been analyzed by scholars including Jennifer Pitts, whose A Turn to Empire (2005) shows how liberal political theory in the 19th century developed justifications for empire that drew on Lockean premises about civilization and improvement. The tension between Locke's universalist rights claims and his colonial applications is not simply a historical curiosity but a structural feature of liberal political thought.
advanced.contestedInheritance: The debate about whether Locke's theory of consent is coherent has been one of the central problems in liberal political theory. Locke's distinction between express and tacit consent — the latter derived from continued residence in a territory — has been criticized as an inadequate basis for political obligation: it seems to generate obligations even where genuine choice is absent. Hume's critique (that tacit consent is not genuine consent when people have no real alternative) remains one of the most important challenges to Lockean political theory.

foundation.theStory: Locke matters as the philosopher who provided the foundational arguments for liberal democracy — consent of the governed, natural rights, limited government, the right of revolution — that the American founders used and that have shaped political thought ever since. Understanding him requires engaging with both his enduring principles and the contradictions his colonial property theory and slave trade involvement represent.
foundation.theDebate: The central debates concern whether his labor theory of property justifies or limits capitalist accumulation (Tully versus Macpherson), whether his philosophy is constitutively connected to colonialism (Arneil and postcolonial critics), whether tacit consent is a coherent basis for political obligation, and how his religious toleration arguments should be extended.
foundation.whyItMatters: Locke matters for understanding liberal democracy, the American Revolution, the history of property rights, the Enlightenment, the philosophical foundations of colonialism, and the tradition of natural rights that underlies contemporary human rights law.
questions: Locke argued that indigenous peoples in America had no property rights in land they had not 'improved' through labor. Is this argument an aberration in his thought or does it reveal something fundamental about the labor theory of property and its relationship to colonialism?

Locke held shares in the Royal African Company while arguing for natural rights. Does this hypocrisy undermine his philosophy, or is the correct response to apply his principles consistently — which would condemn slavery — regardless of his personal practice?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The essential primary texts are the Second Treatise and A Letter Concerning Toleration. Laslett's critical edition of the Two Treatises is the standard scholarly edition. Macpherson's Possessive Individualism is essential for the Marxist critique. Tully's A Discourse on Property for the radical reading. Arneil's John Locke and America for the colonial dimension. Waldron's God, Locke, and Equality for the most rigorous recent philosophical engagement.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The scholarly apparatus for Locke is rich — his manuscripts, correspondence, and published works are extensively edited. The main interpretive challenges are the relationship between the political and epistemological works, the question of what Locke actually meant by consent and property (where the text is genuinely ambiguous), and the relationship between his private activities and his public philosophy.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about Locke must engage with the colonial property argument specifically — not just noting that it exists but analyzing whether it is logically required by the labor theory or an application Locke made that could be rejected. It must take a position on the Macpherson-Tully debate about property. And it must address the consent problem — whether Locke's theory generates genuine political obligations.
questions: Locke argues that revolution is justified when government violates natural rights. But who determines when government has crossed that line? His answer — the people — seems to license endless revolution. How does Locke limit the right of revolution, and is the limitation successful?

Locke's Letter Concerning Toleration excluded Catholics and atheists from toleration. On his own principled grounds — that the state has no authority over conscience — are these exclusions consistent? Or do they reveal that his toleration was more pragmatic than principled?

research.topic: Locke's influence on the American founding
research.content: The influence of Locke's Two Treatises on the American founders is well established through direct textual evidence — the founders' reading lists, their correspondence, the echoes of Lockean language in the Declaration of Independence and constitutional debates. Jefferson's acknowledged debt to Locke, Madison's engagement with Lockean theory in the Federalist Papers, and the broader Whig tradition through which Locke's ideas were transmitted to America are documented. The specific question of how much the founders read Locke directly versus through intermediaries (Sydney, Trenchard, Gordon) has been debated.

research.topic: Locke's relationship to colonialism and slavery
research.content: The scholarly engagement with Locke's colonial property theory and his involvement in the slave trade has substantially developed since Macpherson's initial critique. Arneil's John Locke and America established the centrality of the colonial context to his property theory. More recent work by postcolonial scholars including Charles Mills (The Racial Contract, 1997) has argued that Locke's liberal theory is constitutively connected to racial exclusion rather than incidentally compromised by it. This argument has been contested by liberal Locke scholars who argue that his principles, consistently applied, condemn the applications he made. The debate is active and has significant implications for how liberalism as a tradition relates to its colonial history.

research.topic: Whether Locke knew the Two Treatises were published and coordinated the timing
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: definitive documentary evidence about Locke's role in the publication of the Two Treatises in 1689 — whether he authorized publication, coordinated its timing with the Glorious Revolution, and how much he revised the text before publication. Why partly unavailable: Locke denied authorship of the Two Treatises during his lifetime, which complicates the documentary record; some relevant correspondence has not survived; and the relationship between the manuscripts (dated by Laslett to the early 1680s) and the 1689 publication involves inference as well as documentation. What can be established: the manuscript evidence for dating, the political context, and Locke's denial of authorship. What cannot be fully established: exactly how and why the publication timing was chosen.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Founder of Liberal Democracy
comparativeNarrative.content: The liberal tradition reads Locke as the philosopher who gave liberal democracy its foundational arguments — consent of the governed, natural rights, limited government, separation of powers, religious toleration. On this reading, the American Revolution, the French Declaration of Rights, and ultimately the modern liberal democratic state are all expressions of Lockean principles. The contradictions (slavery, colonial property theory) are failures to apply his own principles consistently rather than revelations of a deeper problem with the principles themselves. Properly understood, Lockean liberalism condemns both slavery and colonial dispossession.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Ideologist of Bourgeois Property
comparativeNarrative.content: The Marxist tradition reads Locke as the ideologist of emergent capitalist property relations — a philosopher whose 'natural rights' arguments naturalized the enclosure of common land, the appropriation of colonial territories, and the accumulation of capital behind the language of universal principle. Macpherson's Possessive Individualism is the foundational text: Locke's political theory assumes a market society in which some people have property and others do not, and it functions to justify this inequality as natural and pre-political. On this reading, liberalism and capitalism are inseparable, and Locke's universalism is the ideological cover for class interest.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Philosopher of Dispossession
comparativeNarrative.content: The postcolonial tradition reads Locke's property theory as the philosophical foundation of colonial land appropriation — the argument that 'unimproved' land could be appropriated by those who would 'improve' it through European-style agriculture was used to dispossess indigenous peoples across the Americas, Australasia, and Africa. Charles Mills's racial contract argument — that liberal political theory was constructed to apply only to white Europeans, with indigenous and enslaved peoples explicitly excluded — extends this critique to liberalism as a tradition. On this reading, the contradiction between Locke's universalism and his colonialism is not incidental hypocrisy but a structural feature of the liberal political tradition.

rabbitHole.label: Thomas Hobbes
rabbitHole.reason: Hobbes and Locke are the two poles of 17th-century social contract theory — Hobbes's absolute sovereignty and Locke's limited government with a right of revolution are the competing answers to the same question about political authority.

rabbitHole.label: The Enlightenment
rabbitHole.reason: Locke's empiricism and political philosophy were foundational for the Enlightenment — Voltaire promoted him in France, his epistemology shaped Enlightenment thinking about human nature, and his political theory provided the vocabulary of natural rights.

rabbitHole.label: The American Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The American founders drew directly on Locke's Two Treatises — the Declaration of Independence's language echoes his natural rights argument, and the constitutional structure reflects Lockean principles of limited government.

rabbitHole.label: Immanuel Kant
rabbitHole.reason: Kant's moral philosophy developed partly in response to the empiricist tradition Locke founded — the Critique of Pure Reason is partly an answer to Humean skepticism that Locke's empiricism had made possible.

rabbitHole.label: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
rabbitHole.reason: Locke and Rousseau are the two most important social contract theorists — their different accounts of the state of nature and political authority define the range of liberal and republican political thought.

rabbitHole.label: The Scramble for Africa
rabbitHole.reason: Locke's labor theory of property — which justified colonial appropriation of 'unimproved' land — provided the philosophical foundation for the colonial land appropriation that the Scramble for Africa exemplified at its most extreme.

rabbitHole.label: Natural Law
rabbitHole.reason: Locke's translation of natural law into the language of individual natural rights — to life, liberty, and property — gave natural law its direct connection to constitutional government and the rights traditions of the democratic revolutions.

reference.author: Locke, John
reference.title: Two Treatises of Government (ed. Peter Laslett)
reference.annotation: The foundational political text — Laslett's critical edition with introduction is the standard scholarly edition, establishing the dating of the manuscript and the political context.

reference.author: Macpherson, C.B.
reference.title: The Political Theory of Possessive Individualism
reference.annotation: The essential Marxist critique — Macpherson's argument that Locke's theory naturalizes capitalist property relations remains the foundational challenge to liberal readings of Locke.

reference.author: Waldron, Jeremy
reference.title: God, Locke, and Equality
reference.annotation: The most rigorous recent philosophical engagement — Waldron argues that Locke's commitment to human equality is deeper and more radical than the standard liberal reading suggests.

reference.author: Locke, John
reference.title: Two Treatises of Government
reference.annotation: The foundational primary source — the Second Treatise contains Locke's account of natural rights, the labor theory of property, and the right of revolution. Essential primary text for liberalism, constitutionalism, and the American founding tradition.

reference.author: Tully, James
reference.title: An Approach to Political Philosophy: Locke in Contexts
reference.annotation: The essential account of the colonial context of Locke's political philosophy — Tully shows that Locke's labor theory of property was developed partly to justify colonial dispossession of indigenous peoples, which changes how we read the canonical liberal text.

commerce.title: Two Treatises of Government
commerce.author: John Locke (ed. Peter Laslett)
commerce.note: Start with the primary source — the Second Treatise especially is readable and short; Laslett's Cambridge edition is the scholarly standard and includes the essential introduction locating the text in its historical context.

commerce.title: Locke: A Very Short Introduction
commerce.author: John Dunn
commerce.note: The best short orientation — Dunn's account emphasizes the theological foundations of Locke's political thought that secular readings miss; essential for understanding why Locke's arguments work the way they do.

commerce.title: The Political Theory of Possessive Individualism
commerce.author: C.B. Macpherson
commerce.note: The essential critical reading — Macpherson's argument that Locke's theory of property naturalizes bourgeois capitalism and excludes the propertyless from full political membership is the most productive left critique; read alongside the Treatises for the productive tension.

commerce.title: John Locke: An Essay Concerning Human Understanding
commerce.author: John Locke (abridged, ed. A.D. Woozley)
commerce.note: For readers who want Locke's epistemology — the Essay is Locke's most philosophically ambitious work and the source of the tabula rasa concept; the Woozley abridgement makes it accessible without losing the argument.

commerce.title: The Ideological Origins of the American Revolution
commerce.author: Bernard Bailyn
commerce.note: The essential account of Locke's American reception — Bailyn shows how the Founding Fathers absorbed and transformed Lockean ideas; essential for understanding how Locke's English liberalism became American constitutionalism.

popularCulture.title: The Declaration of Independence (1776)
popularCulture.description: Jefferson's Declaration is the most consequential application of Lockean political theory in history. 'Life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness' translates Locke's 'life, liberty, and estate' into an American idiom. What it transforms: 'pursuit of happiness' instead of 'estate' — interpreted as softening or expanding the property right, depending on the interpreter.

popularCulture.title: Tabula rasa and the blank slate
popularCulture.description: Locke's argument that the mind at birth is a blank slate on which experience writes is the foundational text of empiricist psychology. The blank slate became one of the most politically loaded claims in modern thought: if human nature is infinitely plastic, the right social arrangements can produce any human type. Steven Pinker's The Blank Slate (2002) is the most systematic recent critique.

popularCulture.title: Separation of powers and the US Constitution
popularCulture.description: Locke's argument for separating legislative and executive power was transmuted by Montesquieu and then by the Founders into the separation of powers doctrine structuring the US Constitution. Contemporary debates about executive power and the administrative state are conducted within a constitutional framework that Locke's political theory helped design.

popularCulture.title: Liberalism as a global ideology
popularCulture.description: Locke is the founding theorist of liberalism: individual rights, limited government, rule of law as foundational. The UN Declaration, constitutional democracy, free markets, and freedom of speech all have Lockean roots. The global spread of liberal institutions raises questions about whether liberalism is a universal philosophy or a specifically English one exported through colonial power.

popularCulture.title: Locke and the justification of colonial dispossession
popularCulture.description: Locke's theory of property was explicitly used to justify the dispossession of Indigenous peoples in North America: Indigenous peoples did not 'improve' their land in the Lockean sense, so European settlers had a superior property right. Locke himself was involved with the Carolina colony and invested in the slave trade. The connection between liberal philosophical foundations and colonial dispossession is among the most important revisionist arguments in political theory.
